title: Mast Cell Disorders
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Hematology
objectives: Differentiate cutaneous mastocytosis from systemic mastocytosis
Apply WHO 2022 diagnostic criteria for systemic mastocytosis
Recognize the role of KIT D816V mutation in diagnosis and treatment
Classify SM subtypes (ISM, SSM, ASM, SM-AHN, MCL)
Manage mast cell mediator symptoms and anaphylaxis risk
Understand treatment options including avapritinib for advanced SM

guidelineReferences.name: Systemic Mastocytosis 2023 Update
guidelineReferences.citation: Pardanani A. Systemic mastocytosis in adults: 2023 update on diagnosis, risk stratification and management. Am J Hematol. 2023;98(7):1097-1116
keyPoints: WHO 2022 criteria: 1 major + 1 minor OR 3 minor criteria
KIT D816V present in >90% of adult SM

guidelineReferences.name: PMC: Evaluation of Mast Cell Disorders

vignette.text:
A 42-year-old woman is referred for evaluation of recurrent flushing, pruritus, and two episodes of anaphylaxis following bee stings (requiring epinephrine). She also reports chronic diarrhea and 'brain fog.' Physical exam reveals scattered tan-brown macules on her trunk that become raised and pruritic when stroked (positive Darier sign).

Past Medical History: Osteoporosis (unusual for age), GERD

Medications: Omeprazole, calcium/vitamin D

Allergies: Bee sting (anaphylaxis x2)

physicalExam.skin: Multiple tan-brown maculopapular lesions on trunk, positive Darier sign (urtication with stroking)
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, mild hepatomegaly on palpation, no splenomegaly

diagnostics.skinBiopsy: Increased mast cells in dermis with aggregates, positive CD117 and CD25

questions.stem: This patient has skin lesions with positive Darier sign and elevated tryptase.
questions.question: How are mast cell disorders classified?

options.text: All mastocytosis is the same disease
Only children get mastocytosis
Skin lesions exclude systemic disease

CUTANEOUS MASTOCYTOSIS (CM): Skin-only involvement, common in children, often resolves by puberty; SYSTEMIC MASTOCYTOSIS (SM): Extracutaneous organ involvement (bone marrow, GI, liver, spleen), requires WHO criteria; MAST CELL ACTIVATION SYNDROME (MCAS): Episodic mediator symptoms, may be clonal or non-clonal

questions.explanation: Mast cell disorder classification: (1) CUTANEOUS MASTOCYTOSIS: Skin-only (urticaria pigmentosa, mastocytoma, diffuse cutaneous), common in children, often self-limited; (2) SYSTEMIC MASTOCYTOSIS: Extracutaneous involvement (bone marrow, GI, liver, spleen), classified by WHO criteria into subtypes; (3) MAST CELL ACTIVATION SYNDROME (MCAS): Episodic symptoms from mast cell mediator release without meeting full SM criteria - can be clonal (monoclonal MCAS) or non-clonal. This adult patient with skin lesions AND elevated tryptase likely has SM - needs bone marrow evaluation.

whyChain.answer: Pediatric mastocytosis: Usually cutaneous only, polymorphic skin lesions, often resolves by puberty, bone marrow biopsy rarely needed unless red flags. Adult mastocytosis: Usually systemic, monomorphic skin lesions, rarely resolves, bone marrow biopsy recommended in ALL adults with suspected mastocytosis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to confirm systemic mastocytosis with bone marrow biopsy.
questions.question: What are the WHO 2022 diagnostic criteria for systemic mastocytosis?

options.text: Bone marrow biopsy is never needed

MAJOR: Multifocal dense MC aggregates (≥15 MCs) in bone marrow or extracutaneous tissue; MINOR: (1) >25% spindle-shaped/atypical MCs, (2) KIT D816V or other activating KIT mutation, (3) Aberrant CD25 and/or CD2 expression, (4) Serum tryptase >20 ng/mL; Diagnosis: 1 major + 1 minor OR 3 minor criteria
Skin lesions are required for diagnosis
Elevated tryptase alone is diagnostic

questions.explanation: WHO 2022 SM Criteria - Need 1 MAJOR + 1 MINOR or 3 MINOR: MAJOR: Multifocal dense MC aggregates (≥15 MCs) in BM or extracutaneous tissue. MINOR: (1) >25% atypical/spindle-shaped MCs in BM smear or tissue; (2) KIT D816V or other activating KIT mutation (high-sensitivity PCR); (3) Aberrant CD25 ± CD2 expression on MCs; (4) Serum tryptase persistently >20 ng/mL (adjust for hereditary alpha-tryptasemia). This patient has elevated tryptase (45 ng/mL) = 1 minor criterion. Bone marrow biopsy needed to confirm diagnosis.

whyChain.why: Why is KIT D816V so important?
whyChain.answer: KIT D816V mutation present in >90% of adult SM. It's a gain-of-function mutation causing constitutive mast cell activation and proliferation. Critical for: (1) Diagnosis - a minor criterion; (2) Treatment selection - D816V confers imatinib resistance but responds to avapritinib/midostaurin. Test using high-sensitivity PCR (standard NGS may miss it).

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Bone marrow confirms SM. You need to classify the subtype.
questions.question: What are the subtypes of systemic mastocytosis?

options.text: Treatment is the same for all subtypes
Skin involvement determines subtype
All SM has the same prognosis

INDOLENT SM (ISM): No B/C findings, good prognosis; SMOLDERING SM (SSM): 2+ B-findings (high MC burden, organomegaly); AGGRESSIVE SM (ASM): 1+ C-findings (organ damage); SM with Associated Hematologic Neoplasm (SM-AHN); MAST CELL LEUKEMIA (MCL): ≥20% MCs in BM aspirate

questions.explanation: SM Subtypes: (1) INDOLENT SM (ISM): Most common (~70%), no B or C findings, normal life expectancy, symptom management; (2) SMOLDERING SM (SSM): 2+ B-findings (high tryptase >200, organomegaly, high BM MC burden), higher progression risk; (3) AGGRESSIVE SM (ASM): 1+ C-findings (cytopenias, hepatomegaly with dysfunction, malabsorption, bone lesions), requires cytoreductive therapy; (4) SM-AHN: SM + associated myeloid neoplasm (MDS, MPN, AML, CMML); (5) MAST CELL LEUKEMIA (MCL): ≥20% MCs in BM, worst prognosis. B-findings = high burden without organ damage. C-findings = organ damage from MC infiltration.

whyChain.answer: This patient has: Elevated tryptase (45, not >200), mild hepatomegaly, osteoporosis. No cytopenias, no liver dysfunction, no ascites. Most likely INDOLENT SM (ISM) - no C-findings, possibly 1 B-finding (hepatomegaly). Bone marrow results will finalize classification. ISM has excellent prognosis with normal life expectancy.

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient's baseline tryptase is 45 ng/mL.
questions.question: How should serum tryptase be interpreted?

options.text: BASELINE tryptase >20 ng/mL is a minor SM criterion; Elevated in SM, myeloid neoplasms, renal failure, anaphylaxis (acute rise); HEREDITARY ALPHA-TRYPTASEMIA (HαT): Extra TPSAB1 gene copies cause elevated baseline - adjust for this; ACUTE tryptase rise: Obtain within 4 hours of anaphylaxis, compare to baseline
Tryptase is only elevated during reactions
Any elevated tryptase confirms mastocytosis
Normal tryptase excludes mastocytosis

questions.explanation: Tryptase interpretation: BASELINE (not during reaction): >20 ng/mL is SM minor criterion, but also elevated in myeloid neoplasms, renal failure, HαT. ACUTE: Rises within 15-60 min of anaphylaxis, peaks 1-2 hours, returns to baseline in 6-24 hours - obtain within 4 hours of reaction and compare to baseline. HEREDITARY ALPHA-TRYPTASEMIA (HαT): Common (5-6% population), extra alpha-tryptase gene copies cause elevated baseline. Adjust: Corrected tryptase = measured/(1 + extra copies). ~20% of SM patients have concurrent HαT. CAVEAT: ~30% of ISM patients have tryptase <20 ng/mL - don't exclude SM based on normal tryptase alone.

whyChain.answer: HαT can cause elevated tryptase WITHOUT mastocytosis, leading to unnecessary workup. However, HαT can also coexist WITH mastocytosis (and worsens symptoms). Test TPSAB1 copy number if elevated tryptase without other SM features, or if tryptase seems disproportionately high for MC burden.

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has had two episodes of bee sting anaphylaxis.
questions.question: How should anaphylaxis risk be managed in mastocytosis?

options.text: VIT should be stopped after 5 years

Mastocytosis patients have INCREASED anaphylaxis risk, especially to Hymenoptera (bee/wasp) stings; Carry epinephrine auto-injectors at all times (at least 2); Venom immunotherapy (VIT) recommended and should be CONTINUED INDEFINITELY; Avoid triggers (NSAIDs, opioids, contrast, alcohol, extreme temperatures)
Anaphylaxis risk is not increased in mastocytosis
Epinephrine is contraindicated

questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis in mastocytosis: INCREASED RISK - Hymenoptera stings cause severe/fatal anaphylaxis more commonly in SM (especially ISM without skin lesions). Management: (1) Epinephrine auto-injectors x2 at all times; (2) VENOM IMMUNOTHERAPY (VIT) strongly recommended - reduces reaction risk by 90%+. CONTINUE VIT INDEFINITELY in mastocytosis patients (unlike general population where 5 years may suffice); (3) Avoid triggers: NSAIDs, opioids, radiocontrast, alcohol, extreme temperature changes, certain anesthetics; (4) Pre-medication for procedures; (5) Medical alert bracelet.

whyChain.answer: In mastocytosis patients, anaphylaxis risk returns when VIT is discontinued (unlike general population where protection often persists). Case reports of fatal reactions after stopping VIT in SM patients. Current guidelines recommend lifelong VIT for mastocytosis patients with Hymenoptera anaphylaxis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to manage this patient's mediator symptoms.
questions.question: How are mast cell mediator symptoms treated in ISM?

options.text: No treatment is available for mediator symptoms
Steroids are first-line for skin symptoms
Chemotherapy is first-line for all symptoms

H1 antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) for pruritus/flushing; H2 antihistamines (famotidine) or PPI for GI symptoms; Cromolyn sodium for GI symptoms; Leukotriene inhibitors (montelukast) for flushing; Aspirin (cautiously, after tolerance testing) for flushing; Omalizumab for refractory symptoms; Avoid known triggers

questions.explanation: ISM symptom management (goal: control mediator release symptoms): (1) H1 ANTIHISTAMINES: Non-sedating (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) for pruritus, flushing - often need higher doses; (2) H2 ANTIHISTAMINES/PPI: Famotidine, ranitidine, omeprazole for GI symptoms, peptic disease; (3) CROMOLYN SODIUM: Mast cell stabilizer, particularly for GI symptoms; (4) LEUKOTRIENE INHIBITORS: Montelukast for flushing, may help other symptoms; (5) ASPIRIN: Can reduce flushing (blocks prostaglandin) but must test tolerance first (can trigger reactions); (6) OMALIZUMAB: For refractory symptoms; (7) OSTEOPOROSIS: Bisphosphonates, vitamin D, calcium. This patient: Start H1 + H2 antihistamines, continue omeprazole, consider cromolyn for diarrhea.

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient has aggressive SM with cytopenias and hepatomegaly with ascites.
questions.question: How is advanced systemic mastocytosis treated?

options.text: Advanced SM has the same treatment as ISM
No targeted therapy exists for SM
Imatinib is first-line for all SM

AVAPRITINIB: Selective KIT D816V inhibitor, FDA-approved for advanced SM (ASM, SM-AHN, MCL), high response rates (75%+); MIDOSTAURIN: Multi-kinase inhibitor, also FDA-approved for advanced SM; CLADRIBINE: Cytoreductive, used if TKIs unavailable; IMATINIB: Only effective if KIT D816V NEGATIVE (rare); Allogeneic HCT for eligible patients with advanced disease

questions.explanation: Advanced SM treatment: (1) AVAPRITINIB: Potent, selective KIT D816V inhibitor, FDA-approved for advanced SM (ASM, SM-AHN, MCL). PATHFINDER trial: 75% ORR, significant reductions in tryptase, MC burden, spleen size. Also approved for ISM (lower dose) for symptom control; (2) MIDOSTAURIN: Multi-kinase inhibitor (KIT, FLT3, PDGFRα), FDA-approved for advanced SM, 60% ORR; (3) CLADRIBINE: Purine analog, cytoreductive; (4) INTERFERON-α: Older option; (5) IMATINIB: Only for D816V-NEGATIVE SM or FIP1L1-PDGFRA+ (rare) - D816V confers resistance; (6) Allogeneic HCT: For eligible patients with refractory/progressive advanced SM.

whyChain.why: Why not imatinib for most SM?
whyChain.answer: KIT D816V mutation (present in >90% of adult SM) causes conformational change that prevents imatinib binding - intrinsic resistance. Avapritinib was designed to specifically target the D816V mutant conformation. Imatinib only works for rare D816V-negative cases or when FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion is present (as in some SM with eosinophilia).

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: No further workup needed
Observation only without treatment

Diagnosis: Bone marrow biopsy with tryptase, CD25, KIT D816V mutation (high-sensitivity PCR); Classification: Likely ISM based on no C-findings; Symptoms: H1 + H2 antihistamines, cromolyn for GI, continue PPI; Anaphylaxis prevention: Epinephrine x2, venom immunotherapy indefinitely, avoid triggers, medical alert ID; Monitoring: Annual tryptase, CBC, LFTs, bone density; Referral: Mastocytosis specialty center
Start chemotherapy immediately

questions.explanation: Complete management: (1) CONFIRM DIAGNOSIS: Bone marrow biopsy with CD117, CD25, tryptase staining; Flow cytometry; KIT D816V by high-sensitivity PCR; Consider HαT testing; (2) CLASSIFY: Based on B/C findings - this patient likely ISM (good prognosis); (3) SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT: H1 antihistamines (cetirizine, consider higher doses), H2 blocker or continue PPI, Cromolyn sodium for diarrhea, Consider montelukast; (4) ANAPHYLAXIS PREVENTION: Epinephrine auto-injectors x2, Venom immunotherapy - START AND CONTINUE INDEFINITELY, Avoid triggers (NSAIDs with caution, opioids, contrast), Pre-medication protocol for procedures, Medical alert bracelet; (5) OSTEOPOROSIS: Already on treatment, continue; (6) MONITORING: Annual tryptase, CBC, LFTs; Repeat bone marrow if progression suspected; (7) REFERRAL: Mastocytosis center for comprehensive care.

sources.text: StatPearls: Mastocytosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected mast cell disorders?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in mast cell disorders. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of mast cell disorders. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing mast cell disorders, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.
